text: This video aims to illustrate a technique, high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery in anterior resection for rectal cancer. Surgeons have concerns whether high ligation of the IMA increases the incidence of anastomotic leakage. Recently, we published a high quality meta-analysis and concluded that high ligation of the IMA did not increase the incidence of anastomotic leakage in anterior resection.

Now we present a video and show how we usually do. A fifty four year old male patient was diagnosed with rectal cancer. The BMI of this patient was twenty eight point two. The colonoscopy showed a two times two centimeters tumor located in thirteen centimeters from the anal verge, and the biopsy pathology with adenocarcinoma. CT did not find distant metastasis.

The surgical plan was laparoscopic-assisted anterior resection with high ligation of IMA and foot apical lymph node dissection. The autonomic nerve would be preserved, and the tumor-specific mesorectum would be excised.

The surgeon excises the apical lymph nodes, followed by ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its root. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and left colic artery (LCA) are ligated together.
The surgeon explores the abdominal cavity to locate the tumor, then separates the left natural attachment and lateral peritoneum. A gauze strip is placed for guidance during the procedure.

The surgeon performs a medial lateral approach, creating tension to facilitate the incision of the peritoneum. Air enters the surgical field, visibly expanding the space and improving clarity for further dissection.
The surgeon separates the talt space using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, taking care to preserve the visible super wire hypogastric plexus during the procedure.

Following the separation of the total space, the surgeon dissects the apical lymph nodes using a harmonic scalpel, carefully protecting the duodenum from damage to prevent post-operative lymphatic fistula.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its root and then separates the attachment between the IMA and the left sympathetic nerve (LSN).
The surgeon further extends the talt space, revealing the gauze strip placed for guidance during the separation of the left natural attachment and lateral peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and left genital vessels. Subsequently, the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and left colic artery (LCA) are ligated together.

The surgeon separates the retrorectal space while preserving the inferior hypogastric plexus. The recto sacrofasta is incised, allowing entry into the superior space of the levator ani muscle. The lateral space of the rectum is carefully separated, ensuring the preservation of the pelvic plexus.
A five-centimeter silk thread is used to mark the location for excision. The surgeon then excises the tumor-specific mesorectum.
The surgeon transects the rectum using a stapler, followed by a visual inspection of the entire epigastric plexus. The surgical specimen is then extracted through a small incision.
The surgeon constructs an anastomosis using the end-to-end double stapling technique, followed by confirmation with an air leak test. A pelvic drainage tube is visibly placed in the surgical field.

The duration of the surgery was one hundred ten minutes and the blood loss was about twenty milliliters. The patient was discharged on day seven. In the thirty day follow-up duration the patient did not develop AL and the genitourinary function was not impaired.
